experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout beneath the gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A dynamic impact array for users on premium donation pages will increase average gift and donor conversion. It encourages greater interaction with the array and helps readers see their gift’s tangible impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/18/2024
One of the best ways to make emails and donation pages feel more personal is by showing donors the real difference their gift can make. In this test, the team added a dynamic callout line beneath the gift array. As supporters clicked through different gift amounts, the message would update in real time to show the impact at that level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a dynamic impact array beneath the gift options led to a significant increase in Transactions, with a 162.3% increase for All Traffic. This indicates that the dynamic callout line effectively encouraged greater interaction with the array and helped donors see the tangible impact of their contributions.

2. Revenue also experienced a substantial increase of 168% with a 98% confidence level.

This suggests that the dynamic impact array not only increased the number of transactions but also positively impacted the average gift amount.
Although on closer inspection, it's clear that the spike in average gift is due to a larger outlier gift, it is still worth noting that no gift surpassed $50 in the control. In contrast, the outlier gift was coupled with multiple gifts between 25 and 50, rather than the typical $10 average gift.
Moving forward, we should continue to consider how the gift array itself can be leveraged alongside this impact statement to achieve the greatest improvement in the average gift.

experiments.title: How a donor goal and exclusivity language in a high-urgency email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a donor goal and exclusivity language for Reason to Believe’s housefile appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it leverages that the donor is part of a special group, and breaks the goal into something they can visualize their impact upon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2024 - 12/03/2024
This year, rather than celebrating Giving Tuesday, Reasons to Believe decided to launch the organization’s very first RTB Giving Day. This was a high-urgency appeal that would last only one-day, with the goal of raising $15,000. We hypothesized that translating this to a 180 donor goal rather than a revenue goal, would increase donor conversion rate for two reasons. First, we believed that it would help underscore these donors as being part of a special group — donors to the first RTB Giving Day; second, we believed that this would help break the more intimidating $15,000 goal into something manageable that the donor could more easily see their own impact in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment illustrate that the use of a donor goal and exclusivity language in the appeal significantly increased the donor conversion rate for Reasons to Believe's RTB Giving Day campaign. This approach helped emphasize the donors' participation in a special group and made the donation goal more manageable and tangible for the donors. The treatment group, which received the donor goal and exclusivity language, saw a 54.0% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence (95.3%).
In future experimentation, we should consider the context around a high-urgency goal and the donor's place in them. The amount of time communicated to reach the goal and perceived difficulty of reaching the goal can all be things that potentially discourage the donor from understanding their impact rather than communicating it. Within the context of a 24-hour appeal and the efforts of RTB to start something unique, it appears that speaking directly to the donors needed to accomplish this was most effective.

experiments.title: How a lowered and reversed gift array impacts monthly donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the gift array with a monthly ask for donors will achieve an increase in recurring gifts because this array is inclusive of more donors as well as realistic to a monthly commitment..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2023 - 10/13/2023
For Reason to Believe’s Monthly Partner campaign, we hypothesized that adjusting the gift array to better align with their monthly ask would increase recurring gifts. In the control, users are primed on the importance of a monthly gift but are met with a gift array that starts $500 and only then decreases to $50 as the lowest monthly option. Our question was whether this could exclude donors who were ready to make a monthly gift but perhaps couldn’t commit to such a large amount and would then abandon the process entirely. By lowering the gift array and reversing it, the starting amount was $10 a month. We believed this better aligned with the monthly commitment and as a result, would increase monthly donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the gift array with a monthly ask can significantly increase recurring donations. The treatment version, which started the gift array at $10 a month instead of $500, saw a 10.9% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version. This suggests that the previous gift array may have excluded potential donors who were ready to make a monthly commitment but couldn't commit to such a large amount. It should be noted that the treatment not only garnered more gifts, but it received all recurring gifts in the experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing and refining the gift array to further optimize recurring donations. This could involve testing different price points and messaging to find the most effective combination. Additionally, it would be valuable to segment the data and analyze the impact of the treatment on different donor groups, such as new donors or existing monthly donors. This information can help tailor the gift array and messaging for different donor segments to maximize the likelihood of recurring donations.

experiments.title: How including an Impact Callout on a Gift Array Impacts Average Gift and Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an impact callout on the $50 amount for potential donors on acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue because donors will be more likely to convert at higher amount if the impact of that gift is outlined.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2024 - 03/27/2024
Reasons to Believe’s paid acquisition efforts are primarily based around free resources. These offers often have no problem gaining traction at the ad level, and they maintain health conversion rates on the landing page and donation page as well. However, a low average gifts has consistently dragged down ROI despite those other data points. After running experiments on the gift array like a “Most Popular” callout to middling results, we identified a different approach for this test. Past wins on RTB’s donation page have often focused the copy around impacting others — we know this audience likes the resources — and are motivated by the prospect of RTB getting them to more people.
Based on this, we hypothesized that highlighting the $50 amount again, but this time with an Impact Callout rather than a “Most Popular” callout would increase average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the impact of a donation can significantly increase average gift size and revenue. By focusing on how the donor's gift can make a difference, we were able to see a 174.5% increase in transactions for all traffic with a 96.1% statistical level of confidence.
Although the original metric targeted for this test, average gift, was relatively similar between control and treatment. The underlying goal of driving revenue and improving ROI was still achieved by the sheer increase in donor conversion. What this indicates is that even if a user could not commit to $50, they were more likely to give still having the impact of a donation to RTB clearly outlined.
In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the impact of donations in our messaging. By clearly outlining how a donor's contribution can benefit others, we can motivate donors to give at higher levels. This approach not only helps increase average gift size but also fosters a stronger connection between donors and the cause, potentially leading to higher donor retention rates.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Donation Page, we hypothesized that a humanized sticky bar treatment featuring an image of RTB’s President Fazale Rana would increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized sticky bar treatment featuring an image of RTB's President Fazale Rana did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing conversion rate. Instead, it resulted in a significant decrease in donations.
This result was unexpected since RTB has used Humanized Sticky Bar of Fazale on the donation page to great success in the past. One change for consideration is in the language between the two sticky bars. In addition to having an image of Fuz, the treatment does change the statement from being one of "we" to "I."
It is possible that this ultimately led the mission to sound more indvidual/Fazale focused rather than broadening to greater impact.

experiments.title: How leveraging specificity and reciprocity on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being specific on the impact of a donor’s gift for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors are not just giving to a cause but making sure that one other person benefits from these resources, which uses the psychological concept of reciprocity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Reasons to Believe was running several ebook offers with an instant donation page that presented the macro mission that the organization addresses. Across all offers, we were seeing a low instant donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that this messaging was not compelling enough for someone to make a first-time donation to RTB. If someone is not familiar with the organization than thinking about their overall mission may not be something they’re bought into yet. We wanted to test creating more specificity in our ask by letting the prospective donor know that we want to get more resources like this into the hands of people who need answers to their questions–will they make it possible so others can access these free resources as well?
This messaging was tested across 3 of their offers that generate the most traffic in order to capture a large enough sample size. Most of the traffic generated to these offers are from paid social ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experimentation is that being specific about the impact of a donor's gift on a potential recipient greatly increases the donor conversion rate. By using the psychological concept of reciprocity, donors feel a sense of obligation to help others benefit from the free resources provided by the organization. In this experiment, the treatment group saw a valid 84.3% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 98%, showing that this messaging resonated well with prospective donors.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue to explore and test messaging that is specific to the impact of a donor's gift. This could include highlighting the number of people that will benefit from a donation or showcasing real-life stories of people who have been positively impacted by the resources provided by the organization.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2022 - 12/16/2022
RTB was running a calendar year end campaign and in our efforts to increase donor conversion rate on the donation page, we decided to run a test where we would would humanize the experience for the prospective donor. So, we added a humanized sticky bar with their president’s picture and message so as to remind the prospective donor that they are giving to people and remind them of the impact of their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results indicate that our hypothesis was correct. We saw a valid 109% lift in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96%. This means that a sticky bar (that had no CTA link or button) that has a personal message from a person (in this case their President), reminds the page visitor that they're not just giving to an organization but to people. Additionally, the reminder of the impact of their gift motivated more people to take action.

experiments.title: How adding a dollar goal to a sticky bar impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the monetary goal in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/31/2022
During a high urgency campaign, RTB ran a sitewide sticky bar that prompted people to give towards their campaign. Mid-campaign the team noted that the stickybar did not state the dollar goal amount and adding this goal may motivate more people to contribute. In order to test this hypothesis we created an experiment where the control (the original sticky bar) did not have the goal amount and the treatment stated the goal amount for the site visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 32% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96%. This means that people were less likely to give when given the dollar amount in the sticky bar. This could be because this makes the experience more transactional for the site visitor. Additionally, it could be that the dollar amount was too large for the prospective donor and it made them feel like it was unattainable (and that their donation would not have an impact).

experiments.title: How emphasizing the value of giving monthly vs incentives impacts click thru rate in a high urgency campaign email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the value of monthly giving rather than the incentives of monthly giving  for prospective donors  will achieve a higher click thru rate and a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign to acquire new recurring donors. Most of the campaign copy was geared towards the incentives for a monthly donor (i.e. special perks, exclusive access, etc.). In one of the last emails of the campaign we wanted to test adjusting the language so that it emphasizes the value of giving monthly vs a 1 time gift. Our hypothesis is that prospective donors will be more motivated to give when they understand why monthly giving is valuable.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 125% valid lift in click thru rate with a 99% level of confidence. This informs us that RTB's audience is more engaged in the email and are more likely to click thru when they're given the value of monthly giving. However, as we dug deeper we saw 11 donations come from the control and 9 donations come from the treatment. This translates into a 18% decrease in donations with a level of confidence of 34.5%. This is not a statistically significant decrease but we were expecting an increase in donor conversion rate. This might be because the actual donation page also had a heavy emphasis on incentives and the messaging of value was not consistent and carried through in the next step.
It's recommended that the donation page copy is tested next time to determine if this language truly motivates more donors to take action.

experiments.title: How focusing on content of an ebook vs the context affected email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the offer as an “ebook”  for site visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2022
RTB has been running an offer called “If God created everything, who created God?” Throughout the promotion, this offer has been couched as a free chapter download of a book. The low sign up rate on the landing page indicated that there was friction on the page that deterred people from signing up. We hypothesized that framing this offer as a free chapter of a book added confusion for the user. If a person is new to the organization and they don’t know the book or the author, including this information on the landing page may be an additional barrier.
For our experiment we decided to exclude this information and simplify the presentation of the offer as just an “ebook.” We believe that the content of the ebook is more important to the end user than the context of the resource (being a chapter from a book).
Traffic to this offer was primarily driven by pad facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 23.6% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 96.7%. Our hypothesis was proven correct: site visitors were more inclined to request this free ebook when we simplified the language and focused on the content of the ebook. While the organization may deem it significant that this is a chapter from a newly released book, someone who is not connected to the ministry may not find that piece of information valuable.

In order to maximize results from this traffic we wanted to add a dear reader at the end of the blog content to capture more names. We wanted to test how humanizing the dear reader by making it sound like it’s coming from an authority figure (Hugh Ross, founder and President) would impact email acquisition. experiments.keyLearnings: Results of this experiment indicate that RTB's audience are more likely to respond when hearing from a specific person rather from the organization. experiments.title: How adding a donor countdown on a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal and donor countdown on a sticky bar  for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2022 - 05/08/2022
RTB was running a recurring giving (Monthly Partner) campaign. Their goal was 100 new recurring donors by the end of the campaign. In order to bring awareness to the campaign, the used a sticky bar on their website to drive traffic to the Monthly Partner donation page. While they’ve been using “100 monthly donors” in their messaging, we hypothesized that breaking down the goal into a smaller, more achievable goal would drive more donations. We stated that “10 monthly donors” are needed today in order to provide a micro-goal. In addition to that change, we had the button have an active countdown on how many monthly donors were left in the daily goal.
Traffic to this page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 100% increase in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 95%. However through most of the duration, the experiment was targeted to only the homepage (not site-wide), which gave us a smaller sample size than expected. Nonetheless, the experiment did validate and informs us that RTB's audiences are more likely to respond to a smaller, achievable goal in a recurring donor campaign. The larger goal of 100 donors can potentially feel like a daunting goal that the donor cannot realistically contribute to.
After this experiment ran, we launched a site-wide pop up with both the micro goal and the donor countdown. This is worth retesting for a different high urgency campaign to see if we get the same type of response.

RTB has typically always had a a premium on their main donate page that cycles out every month and/or quarter. We wanted to test removing the premium offer to see how this change would impact donations and overall revenue. We thought that the premium ask may be overwhelming visitors, leading to anxiety and friction. Furthermore, we wanted to see if perhaps RTB did not need a premium at all as an incentive. Would prospective donors still give if they did not have a premium option. If the difference was negligible, the case could be made that the premium isn’t always needed and there could be additional cost saved for the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 69.5% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence. The results indicate that RTB's audience is highly motivated by the premium offer on the donation page. With a 100% confidence, we know that their constituents are conditioned to expect the premium on the donation page and are more likely to give when it is featured. We have concluded to keep the premium on the donation page. Given that the premium is such a strong driver, it is recommended that we add the premium option to the instant donation pages to their offers that have a low ROI.

Using the eBook that was related to the author gave us a valid 50% lift in email conversion rate. This is consistent with the previous experiment's (Hugh Ross' blog/dear reader) findings. In addition to a lift in email conversion, we also see a lift in donations and revenue. However, it is still unclear as to whether the lift was caused by the author relevance or the fact that the ebook is simply a better offer than the survey. We are following up with an experiment that uses the ebook dear reader and uses first person language so it sounds like it's from the author himself.

experiments.summary:

Reasons to Believe moved to a new donation platform and had some limitations in their page design elements. In addition to these complications, there have been adjustments made to their man donation page over time as needs arose within the organization. This meant that elements, fields, and placements were added out of necessity. We also saw that the placement of different page elements were skewed based on the size of the screen. We hypothesized that these changes could be affecting their revenue. We wanted to implement a radical redesign experiment where we could address all these issues.
Traffic to this main donate page came primarily through organic traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organization experienced a valid revenue lift of 81%. These findings tell us that the initial donation page had a lot of friction for the user. It can be hypothesized that even the layout of the copy (which was crowded and oddly spaced on different screen sizes initially), drew the prospective donor to read the value prop outlined and inspired larger gifts. Adding elements like the box and lock icon over the payment method was also introduced as way to improve results.
It should also be noted that their donation pages were experiencing captcha issues that were stalling out donation submissions. There was also a fix implemented on this page through this experiment. The impact of this fix is unknown.
Next steps include:
testing this new layout on their offers' instant donation pages. Isolating the captcha issue into its own experiment in order to determine revenue/conversion impact

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results should not be used as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How exclusivity language on a high-urgency pop-up impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including exclusivity language for users on the RTB website will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it highlights the donor’s identification with a group, beyond just being a donor..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s first-ever Giving Day, we hypothesized that the inclusion of exclusivity language — highlighting that the donor is joining a unique or exclusive group of donors to the first RTB Giving Day — will improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including exclusivity language on the RTB website did lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

While the initial findings are promising and suggest that exclusivity language may indeed impact donor conversion rate positively, we should not make any definitive conclusions or implement permanent changes based on these results alone. Further testing with a larger sample size and longer duration is needed to validate the impact of exclusivity language on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How guided reading subheaders impact donor conversion on e-book donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that guided reading subheadings for users on an instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because increases readability of the copy while also clearly imparting the problem, solution, and how the donor’s gift makes a difference. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2024 - 11/19/2024
We believe that guided reading subheadings (that outline a problem, solution, and what you can do) on instant donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although it would appear that guided reading subheadings on instant donation page can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The statistical level of confidence was only 80.%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. In addition, the sample size is very small with a total of 5 transactions in the test.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence, ideally above 85%. This will help to ensure that the results are reliable and can be used to make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.

experiments.title: How using the concept of autonomy and donor volition impacts giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because we focused less on urgency/pressure and more on value .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted shift the messaging of a campaign email to be less urgency/pressure focused and more autonomy driven – giving the donor the choice to give/not give. This is based on autonomous (volitional) motivation – donors give because they personally understand and value the cause; it’s their way to enact and share their values. This test was a complete copy overhaul and was tested across 2 of the last emails of a high urgency campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients led to a significant decrease in donations compared to the urgency/pressure focused messaging; a directional 46% decrease in donor conversion rate with a LoC of 93%. This indicates that the shift in messaging strategy did not resonate as effectively with donors as intended.

Additionally we experienced a valid 16% decrease in clicks, indicating that the messaging did not resonate enough with recipients to even click through the donation page.

Given that these are the last 2 emails of a campaign, where urgency is what typically drives action, it may be that RTB's audience is primed and expectant of this type of messaging as they approach the deadline.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a content offer email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including social proof in a content offer email for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it reinforces the perceived value of the offer based on what others are already saying about it..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2024 - 02/14/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s resend of the Genesis 1 e-book offer to their housefile, we hypothesized that since this offer has already run previously including a line of social proof in the email to generate interest would impact sign-up rate and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inclusion of social proof in the content offer email for the housefile led to a significant increase in donor conversions. The statistical level of confidence was at 88.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing is recommended for certainty.

What's notable is that although this experiment only took place at the email level, however the primary metrics impacted were downstream in the funnel. The line of social proof's impact on click rate was negligible, with a 1.2% lift at a 16.1% LOC. However, those who clicked from the social proof treatment were far more likely to sign up and far more likely to give with a 14.3% increase in sign-ups on the landing page and a 59.6% in donor conversions.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential effectiveness of social proof in driving donor conversions. By incorporating this strategy into future email campaigns and conducting further testing, Reasons to Believe can continue to optimize their efforts to attract and convert donors effectively.

experiments.title: How posing a lower, $10 ask for non donors impacts email engagement and giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a specific, lower ask amount for non donors will achieve an increase in click-through rates and ultimately gifts because having a specific, low amount can reduce decision friction and potential anxiety.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2023 - 12/22/2023
During calendar year end, we wanted to activate more nondonors on RTB’s file. In order to lower the threshold for those who have never given, we posed a $10 ask as a test. The control received no specific amount while the treatment received a $10 ask in their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a specific, lower ask amount of $10 to non-donors can result in an increase in click-through rates compared to not mentioning a specific amount. The treatment version of the email showed a 20.5% increase in clicks for all traffic with a level of confidence of 87%.

Additionally, only two gifts were generated from this email both of which came from the treatment and  both at an amount of $25 which is higher than the initial proposed amount.

It's suggested that this test is ran again with the $10 ask as the starting point of the gift array that the treatment group receives. That way, there's further congruency in the ask and can potentially activate more donors.

experiments.title: How Turning Campaign Graphics into a Plain-Text Design Affects Conversion on Donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain-text donation page rather than stylized campaign graphics for RTB’s FYE campaign  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it positions the copy/value as being from a human..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 07/03/2023
For Reason to Believe’s Fiscal Year End campaign, they had created an array of graphics illustrating their goals and the impact of their Scholar Community. We hypothesized that removing these graphics on the donation page in favor of a plain text design would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. Note that any copy in the images was translated into bullet points for the plain-text treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Plain-text donation pages are more effective in converting donors compared to stylized campaign graphics. The treatment group experienced a 332.0% increase in donations, indicating that the plain-text design resonated better with potential donors. The plain-text design may have created a more personal and relatable experience for visitors, leading to higher engagement and donations.
The statistical level of confidence for the increase in donations is 93.2%, which is approaching the 95% threshold for validity. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to ensure the results are consistent and reliable — the test should also have a greater sample size than the 7 donations for validity.
Based on these learnings, it is recommended to continue testing and refining the plain-text donation page design. Further experimentation can be conducted to validate the results and explore additional factors that may influence donor conversion rates.
Overall, this experiment provides valuable insights for improving donor conversion rates through the use of plain-text design and a more human-centric approach. By continuing to iterate and test, the organization can optimize its donation page for better fundraising outcomes.

experiments.title: How a Lower Suggested Gift and Array Impacts Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array and reversing it to start low to high for users on the Designed to the Core premium will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lowered “entry level” will appeal to a wider variety of donors versus excluding those who may not be ready to give a $500 gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s donation pages go from highest to lowest, starting at a high of $500 and then decreasing to the suggested gift of $50. We hypothesized that reversing this array to go from low to high as well as lowering the four amounts will improve the donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which involved lowering the gift array and reversing it to start low to high, showed a significant increase in donor conversion rate compared to the control version.
2. The treatment resulted in a 163.2% increase in donations for all traffic.
3. The statistical level of confidence reached 88.1%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but are not yet considered fully valid.
4. Further testing would be necessary to confirm if the treatment has the intended effect with a higher level of confidence.
Based on these learnings, it is clear that the changes made in the treatment version resonated with donors. The lower entry level and the reversed order of the gift array likely appealed to a wider variety of potential donors, attracting those who may not have been ready to give a $500 gift.
To continue experimenting in the future, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate the effectiveness of the treatment. This could involve increasing the sample size, extending the duration of the experiment, or implementing additional variations to the gift array. By doing so, the organization can gain more confidence in the impact of these changes and make informed decisions about optimizing its donation pages to maximize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How low-to-high gift array affected donor conversion (Adam and Eve)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the Adam and Eve instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start. We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that offering a low-to-high gift array for users on the Adam and Eve instant donation page did not increase donor conversion rate, as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 69.8% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 93.3% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the audience and context of the donation page. It may be that for certain causes or types of donors, a low-to-high gift array is not effective, but for others, it may be more successful. Therefore, further testing is needed, with a focus on identifying the specific factors that lead to successful donation pages. Additionally, it may be helpful to test multiple variations of gift arrays to determine the most effective approach for each audience and context.

experiments.title: How adding first name personalization to a donation page affected donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by personalizing the headline with a first name for survey participants  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2022 - 07/30/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a survey offer that directed users to an instant donation page after completion. We wanted to see an increase in the instant donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that by personalizing the headline with a first name it would create a greater connection with the reader and communicate a deeper appreciation for their participation. Additionally, we suspected that the control headline was communicating to the user that there was no further action to be taken: “Thank you for your insights and for giving your opinion.” Without changing the primary message, we believed using a personalization would make for a headline that is less likely to be ignored.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for 50 days, and saw a 124% increase in donor conversion rate with a 83.5% confidence level.
The level of confidence and small low number of donations (preferably at least 50) still encourages room for testing. There are a few notable hints in the data regarding our initial concept. Our hypothesis was that a name personalization would show greater appreciation and stand out to the user. One interesting comparison is that the treatment caused little to no change in conversion rate for new visitors, but did cause a slight increase for returning visitors. But alternatively, Organic Search visitors had a 67% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence. Is there a learning to be had on when users perceive the most value in personalization? Is there thought to be more value in someone "remembering" our name, than an organization using it directly after it was provided?
Ultimately, more testing is still required in order to validate these findings.
Revenue experienced a 85.0% decrease with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 17.1% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 9.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 14.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 66.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How making a headline more than just a thank you/confirmation on instant donation page affects results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by better aligning the headline copy with the body copy on the instant donation page for users completing the survey will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2022 - 10/27/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a survey offer that directed users to an instant donation page after completion. The body copy told a testimony of someone who was able to reconcile their doubts between faith and science through RTB’s resources. The ask was that their generosity is what makes this possible, and would continue to bring more skeptics back to the faith.
We wanted to see an increase in the instant donor conversion rate. We believed this number was low because users were not actually reading the testimony, but simply exiting because the headline didn’t communicate that there was more to do. The original read, “Thank you for giving your insights and for giving your opinion.” We replaced this with a headline that we believed would encourage the user to read on, “Thank you for your insights. We also want to see more skeptics come to Christ. Here’s why your response matters.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for 3 months, but could not reach an adequate sample size of at least 50 donations to determine validity. The result was that the treatment actually resulted in a 68.1% decrease in donations with an 85.7% confidence level. However, it should be noted that the entirety of this decrease seemed to result from mobile traffic. The desktop view showed a 1.3% increase in conversion rate (though with low confidence), meanwhile, the numbers on mobile view accounted for 84% of the 85.7% decrease at a nearly valid level of confidence (94.5%).
Aside from the change in messaging, the biggest difference between the treatment and the original is that the treatment is twice as long. If the problem was the actual message/value of the headline, one would think that it would cause a similar decrease whether read on desktop or on mobile. In other words, if the problem was an uncompelling sentence, it would stand to reason that it would be uncompelling whether you read it on small screen or on a big one.
This is not the case. Instead, the treatment didn't show any decrease in the desktop view. 84% of the 85% decrease came solely from mobile users. It's possible that by doubling the length of the headline, it increased friction for mobile users by eliminating the body copy from view unless they scrolled down. It appears that most of these mobile users were also new visitors. Meaning that if there appeared to only be a headline with no body copy beneath it,  a majority of these users had no prior experience to make them think otherwise.
The key learning is more of a reaffirmation: mobile traffic continues to be a huge factor in your results and the mobile experience should always be tested and considered.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 82.2% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 68.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had an 84.0% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.New visitors had an 81.1% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an email acquisition form directly to a relevant blog page impacted offer sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an offer sign up opportunity directly below a related and important blog article for page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2022 - 08/04/2022
Reasons to Believe built an offer in which users could welcome the organization’s newly appointed CEO by submitting an “encouraging note” to him. The offer was set up in its traditional way: After clicking on an ad or email link, users would go to a landing page where they would read about this opportunity to submit a personal note. They could then write the note and submit it after providing their first name, last name, and email address.
RTB also posted a blog article on the news of naming the successor to their longstanding president and CEO. We wanted to test how adding the same acquisition form below the blog content would impact email submissions. So instead of reading about the opportunity to submit a personal note, users would read about the news through the blog article and be given the opportunity to submit the personal note afterwards.
For the treatment, we added a bolded call-to-action after the blog article and then added the same acquisition form as we used in the control. We wanted to test which presentation of the offer would lead to more email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 60% lift in email conversions but only with a 72.5% level of confidence. The experiment did not validate. This offer could only run for a limited amount of time since it was news related. But it might be worth testing again in a similar capacity to see if there is in fact a trend in email conversion for a blog-level acquisition opportunity.
The experiment also revealed that returning visitors were twice as likely to convert than new visitors, which tells us that returning visitors are more familiar with RTB blogs and would perhaps get more out of reading a blog article about a new president taking over for the former president. If a similar offer is run again, it could be tested further for returning visitors.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook offer to a survey funnel impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ebook offer in a funnel  for survey takers will achieve a higher instant donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2022 - 06/20/2022
Reasons to believe was running an offer that encouraged people to take a science faith survey. After taking the science faith survey, they were directed to an instant donation page. Seeing that they had a low instant donor conversion rate, we wanted to add an additional step in the funnel that would provide additional value for the user. We selected one of their high performing ebook, Genesis 1, as an additional offer we can provide. The new funnel would lead people to the ebook landing page after they filled out the survey. Once they filled out the ebook form, they were led to an instant donation page. Our hypothesis that by giving the survey taker additional value in the form of an ebook, they would be more likely to give.
This was ran as a redirect experiment as 2 separate funnels. The traffic to this funnel was generated primarily through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the results of this experiment did not validate we did see a near directional 75% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 82%. While there weren't many donations in this experiment it ran for nearly 2 weeks and was not gaining much traction. It's possible that RTB's audience may have already received this ebook and did not take action in downloading it. As a result, they did not get to the instant donation page and had no opportunity to give. It may be worth testing this on some sort of prospecting channel where it is more unlikely that anyone has encountered this specific ebook. It's possible that the donation page itself needs further testing to motivate survey takers to donate.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook incentive in a survey impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ebook incentive to a survey for survey takers will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2022 - 06/10/2022
RTB launched a new offer: Science and Faith Survey. Surveys have historically produced a lower sign up rate than other offers like ebooks. In order to provide additional incentive to provide contact information as well as provide additional value for the site visitor, we included a checkbox at the end of the survey that, if checked, will send them a free ebook. Our hypothesis is that this extra added incentive and value, will increase email acquisition.
Traffic to this offer was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, we did see a directional 13% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 93%. This is counter to what our initial hypothesis had stated. A possibility is that some users saw this as an extra step in their process instead of an added incentive and therefore did not perceive the ebook as an added value. However, looking at these users at the instant donation page step, the users who downloaded the ebook were more likely to take action on the instant donation page. In fact, we saw a directional 383% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. This tells us that while many did not take advantage of the added incentive, those who understood the value of this ebook were more motivated to give to Reasons to Believe after they submitted their survey responses.
Potential next steps include:
Prechecking the box for the ebook; checking the box may be adding additional friction and more "work" for the end userAdding an image of the eBook to reinforce the added value Add another step after the survey to get the ebook in order to increase the donor conversion rate

experiments.title: Does adding value proposition language affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign-ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared calmer and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.

Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: How an ascending gift array affects revenue on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an ascending gift array for potential donors will achieve higher revenue per donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2021 - 08/27/2021
RTB has typically had a gift array on their donation pages with descending amounts, meaning the largest amount is the first option on their donation page. On RTB’s main donation page, we wanted to test reordering the gift array, updating it from decreasing amounts to increasing amounts. We wanted to see how it impacted revenue – would it lead people to give higher amounts? And how would it impact overall giving?
experiments.keyLearnings: Results indicated a 46.9% directional decrease in revenue (level of confidence 88%). While a typical gift array is set up in an ascending order, RTB's audiences seem to respond more to the descending order already put in place. Although the results are not statistically valid, we did see a directional  decrease after a month of testing. It is possible that RTB's audiences are already conditioned to respond to their gift array order. When observing donor conversion rate there is even less statistical significance (10% difference at 50% level of confidence). It is recommended that this is retested on the instant donation pages of offers as those funnels tend to have more newer users which may respond differently than RTB's current audience.

experiments.title: Does creating more clarity in the text of a landing page affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared passive and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
Traffic to this free online course primarily came through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a directional 16% decrease in email acquisition (level of confidence 93%). We had theorized that the extra detail regarding the course were a point of friction for landing page visitors but the data seems to indicate that RTB's audience is actually looking for these details to make their decision.
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.
Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: How moving the placement of the premium affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the placement of the premium for prospective donros will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2022 - 03/06/2022
RTB was running a direct appeal ad centered around a premium. In order to increase donor conversion rate we wanted to move the placement of the premium image. Having it above the fold can possibly inspire more conversions as people see what they’re getting in exchange for their donation
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we assumed that changing the placement of the book would increase donor conversion, it seems as if the control was the preferable placement. While the experiment did not validate, we did see a directional decrease in donor conversion rate. However, the traction on this offer was slow (only 3 donations to account for), so we recommend retesting this concept on a page with more traffic.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a dynamic thermometer on the donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic thermometer for users on the Giving Day donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it helps underscore the urgency of this single-day appeal and helps the donor better visual their role in reaching the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s Giving Day, we hypothesized that including a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the $15,000 goal during this one-day appeal would help generate greater urgency and improve donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The addition of a dynamic thermometer showing the progress of the fundraising goal on the donation page had a significant impact on donor conversion rates, with a 80.6% increase in donations for all traffic.

2. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 62.9% is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%, which means that the results may not be considered completely reliable.

3. It is important to recognize that while the treatment was successful in this particular instance, the results cannot be referenced as a basis for future decision-making due to the low statistical confidence level.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide a higher level of statistical confidence before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the results of the experiment. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further tests or iterations of the dynamic thermometer feature to validate its impact on donor conversion rates before making it a permanent fixture on the donation page.

experiments.title: How simplified value prop on a premium donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop for users on a premium donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it introduces them to the impact of their gift regarding the premiums specifically, rather than a more general “org-centered” value prop.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/23/2024
On Reasons to Believe’s premium donation pages, we noticed that multiple pages had an old value prop (one now replaced in newer copy) that outlined a series of bullet points demonstrating the impact of a gift. This language tended to be longer, more focused on how the org is impacted and the full mission of RTB, versus the newer premium specific value prop which simply talked about the impact of a donor’s gift regarding the premium specifically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that simplifying the value proposition on a premium donation page can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with a more focused and specific value prop regarding the impact of the donor's gift on premiums saw a 61.7% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 67.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to conduct similar tests with a larger sample size to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the value proposition to see if there are specific language or messaging that resonates best with donors on premium donation pages. By continuously testing and iterating on different approaches, we can gather more robust data and make more informed decisions to optimize donation conversions.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array options for monthly gift frequency affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because having different options for a one-time and monthly gift will reduce their decision friction and give clarity on the type of gift and amount they want to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/19/2024
For Reasons to Believe, they typically have the same gift array options for both one-time and recurring gift frequencies. For this test, we wanted to test changing the gift array options for the monthly frequency to have options that would be more suitable for a monthly gift. We also changed the placement of the options to select the gift frequency to be above the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options above the gift array did not have the desired effect of increasing donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.8% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic with a 5.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. Therefore, it is inconclusive whether the changes made had any impact on donation behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the design of the gift array options carefully and ensure that any changes made are based on solid research or data-driven insights. It may also be beneficial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How using a video on a high urgency campaign donation page impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the transcript of a video on a donation page for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/17/2022
Reasons to Believe launched their fiscal year end campaign and in their launch email they were leading their email recipients to a donation page with a video. Past experiments with other organizations have shown that a video on a donation page can actually add friction to the giving experience. The reason being that many people are unlikely to watch through a whole video and they’re more likely to read through some copy.
For this email we sent half of their list to a donation page with the video (the control) and the other half of the list to a donation page with a transcript of the content of the video (the treatment). Our hypothesis is that more people would convert on the donation page with the transcript of the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 56% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%. Something to note is that on the email itself, RTB used different copy on the CTA button. The control had "Watch my video" and the treatment had "Read my note." When looking at the click thru rate between each button we saw a valid 28% decrease in clicks with a level of confidence of 99%. In a sense, 2 variables were tested in this experiment: the call to action and the donation page itself.

It's likely that RTB's audience is conditioned to watch videos for their content and throughout their high urgency campaigns. However it seems as more traffic was pushed to the video donation page because of the different CTA. Watching a video may sound more appealing than to read something.

It's recommended that this is retested in a channel that gets more traffic and that has the same CTA button, like a sticky bar. This will isolate one variable and get us more definitive results.

experiments.title: How clarity around the problem and expanded value prop on main donate impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increase clarity around the problem and expanding value prop copy to specify how RTB is the solution for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because users will not only have better clarity around the problem RTB solves but also the impact a gift makes towards solving it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/26/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s main donation page, we believe there was an opportunity to leverage the value prop language we’d seen perform well on acquisition donation page and in campaigns. We hypothesized that adding more clarity around what the problem is and better illustrating how a gift to RTB helps solve it would improve conversion rate. A few specific callouts are the more action-oriented headline versus “Support Reasons to Believe. The inclusion of details about what types of questions RTB answers, whose writing the resources, and finally what’s really at stake when these questions go unanswered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing clarity around the problem and expanding the value proposition copy to specify how Reasons to Believe addresses the users' needs can significantly impact donor conversion rates and revenue generated. While donor conversion rate remained similar between control and treatment, the latter did show a substantial 103.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.5%, below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing variations of the value proposition copy and messaging to determine the most effective way to communicate the impact of donations to Reasons to Believe. Additionally, conducting experiments with larger sample sizes or running the test for a longer period may help increase the statistical confidence level of the results. Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that focusing on clearly articulating the problem and how RTB provides a solution can have a positive impact on donor conversions.

experiments.title: How a Humanized Sticky Bar Impacts Conversion Rate on the Homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors are more likely to give to a real person than a general organizational mission..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Sticky Bar, we hypothesized that a humanized version from RTB’s President Fazale Rana would increase donor conversion rate. We believe that having the campaign mission come from a real person will allow for a better connection with the donor and help reinforce that these are not the goals of just an “organization” but individuals seeking to make an impact as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized version of the sticky bar, featuring language from the organization's President, did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control version. However, these results did not validate. However, they do prompt closer consideration of the language.
Does the change of "we" to "I" have a potential impact on how the statement is read for the donor? The goal of incorporating an image of Fuz was to humanize the mission, but it's possible that the copy inadvertently made the mission Fuz focused rather than communicating the broader goal.
It is important to continue experimenting and testing different strategies to optimize donor conversion rate. Future experiments could explore different messaging techniques or other elements of the website to find more effective ways to engage donors and increase conversions.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language and a Countdown Impacts Conversion Rate on a CYE Pop Up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using urgency language and countdown for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it underscores the limited window of time available for the donor to make their gift..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Pop-Up, we hypothesized that an added line of copy for urgency and a countdown time would increase donor conversion rate. The treatment iteration added “We urgently need your help.” to the copy as well as the dynamic countdown. Aside from these changes, the body copy remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding urgency language and a countdown to the homepage did not increase donor conversion rate, however the level of confidence was far below the amount needed for validity.
It should be noted that originally this popup was supposed to a include an end-of-year deadline for a matched gift opportunity. This was met shortly before the popup launched and the line about the match had to be removed. With this tangible deadline missing, it does prompt the question "What is urgent?" Yes, the mission stated in the copy is undoubtedly important, but does having a countdown on that mission of just 7 days end up seeming overreactive to the user?
In future experiments, it would be beneficial to run a countdown for a longer period and in a context that still has a clear deadline for the user to correlate alongside the timer.

experiments.title: How a low-to-high gift array affects donor conversion (Why the Bible)
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the (Why the Bible) instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
because  highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start.  We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting a more entry-level gift amount on an instant donation page can potentially increase the number of first-time donors and those who cannot gift a large amount but still want to support. This hypothesis was supported by the treatment group, which saw a 64.7% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making. However, testing this same treatment on a page with greater sample size could help this hypothesis validate.

experiments.title: How a low-to-high gift array affected donor conversion (Noah’s Flood)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the Noah’s Flood instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start. We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 24 days, and the results showed that the treatment group saw a 48.1% increase in revenue for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 73.4%. Though these results cannot be considered valid.
From this experiment, the key learnings are that highlighting a more entry-level gift amount increased the conversion rate for first-time donors and those who are unable to gift a large amount but want to support the cause. Furthermore, it is evident that the organization should continue testing and experimenting to find out the ideal donation page design and gift array.
In future experimentation, the organization should increase the sample size, use a longer testing period and improve the test variation to ensure higher statistical validity

experiments.title: How Pulling Mobile Give Button Out of Menu Impacts Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the donate button visible in the navigation bar for for mobile users will achieve an increase in donations  because  the increased visibility eliminates friction for those who either might not have known where to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s Mobile view hides the Donate button within the “hamburger” menu. So, unlike the desktop view, a user has to open the menu then select “Donate” from the button in order to make a gift rather than just clicking a button in the navigation. We believe that updating this, so that the give button is displayed for mobile view will increase donations.
Additionally, we ran this test during fiscal year end know that there would be more website traffic and motivation would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the donate button visible in the navigation bar for mobile users resulted in a 35.1% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 61.1%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for the results to be considered valid and reliable. Additionally, we did not meet the recommended 50 transaction minimum even after running this experiment for a little over a month.
Therefore, while there is some indication that making the donate button more visible could increase donations, further experimentation is needed to confirm these findings.
Given when this test was ran (during fiscal year end), it's also likely that the motivation to give was too high and this treatment made no difference.
It's recommended we retest this concept with a different approach. We want to highlight the donate call to action but may need additional value proposition language to give the site visitor more context as to why they should give.

experiments.title: How Lowered Gift Array on Main Donate Impacts Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array and displaying it as low to high for users on the general donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it lowers the “level of entry” for general users on the main donate page who might not be familiar with RTB..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s Main Donate page displays a high to low gift, with $500 being the first option and the suggested low amount being $50. We hypothesized that reversing this, lowering it, and suggesting an amount of $25 will increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Changing the gift array from high to low and suggesting a lower amount did not result in an increase in donor conversion. The treatment version of the experiment saw a 16.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that the control version performed better in terms of conversion.
However, the statistical level of confidence for the decrease in donations for all traffic is 68.0%, which is below the 85% threshold. This was not the expected result, since this performed well on a Premium donation page and was expected to perform just well if not better on a general page. This assumption was partly rooted in that typically general donation page might have more "new users" but in reality the traffic to this page shows that RTB still receives a lot of returning visitors.
More testing is needed in order to achieve a validated learning from this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding goal language to a sticky bar impacts donors conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more specificity in a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more urgency is communicated by the goal language .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
RTB was running a fiscal year end campaign and implemented a site-wide sticky bar to generate more gifts. In order to increase donor conversion, we added more specificity and urgency to the sticky bar by adding the goal amount and a time to the deadline. The hypothesis is that the urgency language and specific dollar amount will drive more motivation to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more specificity and urgency to a sticky bar did not achieve the desired result of increasing donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version actually saw a decrease in donations. We saw a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below 85%, which means the results are not considered valid.
However, based on the data collected, it may be worth exploring other factors that could have influenced the decrease in donations. It is possible that the addition of the specific dollar amount and deadline in the treatment version may not capture new donors who need more context for the campaign. In fact, the control brought in 6 new donors while the treatment only brought in 2 new donors.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different variations of the sticky bar, focusing on other value propositions for the campaign.

experiments.title: How adding urgency in a pop up during fiscal year end impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding language that communicates urgency for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the time-bound offer will drive donors to give now as opposed to later .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
During RTB’s high urgency campaign they wanted to drive urgency on the site by implementing a pop up to give during the last few days of the campaign. In order to drive the urgency we developed a treatment that used urgency language to drive fast action among prospective donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing language that communicates urgency did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate for the nonprofit's high urgency campaign. On the contrary, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations. There was a 37% decrease in donor conversion but only with a level of confidence of 59%.
