Because this only ran for a few days and only generated 13 donations, it's recommended that this retested in a longer time frame.

experiments.title: Will asking for a larger specific amount increase donations and average gift size
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking for a larger specific donation amount for a smaller donor set will achieve an increase donations and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2021
We wanted to test if sending a direct ask email mentioning a matching gift and asking for a small number of donors to donate a larger amount VS the standard direct ask mentioning the match would help increase the average gift as well as overall revenue.
So the control email mentioned the match and asked for a gift while the treatment mentioned the match and asked for 10 donors to make a gift of $1,000.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the control email performed well and received 54 transactions for $11,737 with an average gift of $217.35 the treatment email saw a smaller number of donations with only 20 transactions but saw a significant increase in revenue and average gift. The total amount raised in the treatment email was $16,745 for an average gift of $837.25!

From this, we see that asking for a smaller number of donors with a larger donation amount can provide exclusivity and an urgency to donate now.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a premium of Navigating Genesis for users who already downloaded OT questions for free will achieve a higher ROI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2022 - 01/26/2023
RTB was running a free offer for their Old Testament Questions eBook, in an effort to improve the ROI we tested a premium offer for another ebook, Navigating Genesis on the instant donation page for Old Testament Questions. For a gift of any amount, the donor would receive a copy of this premium, Navigating Genesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Instead of an increased ROI, we actually saw a directional decrease in the average gift but with a total of only 9 donations, this experiment does not validate. A potential area to test is the exact wording of the premium offer. The options are either to be sent the Navigating Genesis offer, OR what is pre-selected is "No, I'd like to maximize my gift." My thought is that this creates conflict in the donor's mind of what their action should be.
On one hand, there's this generosity of the organization saying "we want to thank you with a gift," but also insinuating that they shouldn't accept it if they actually want their donation to be maximized. As a result, the donor begins weighing what they should actually do, deliberating over it too much and just deciding to exit due to the friction.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 75.4% decrease in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 73.0% decrease in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Email visitors had an 83.3% decrease in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How giving is impacted by the use of buttons in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation buttons from an email appeal for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
RTB historically uses multiple “Give Now” buttons in email appeals. During calendar year end we decided to run an experiment where we removed the buttons from the email appeal. The idea is that the removal of the buttons would actually humanize the communication more, make the email seem less promotion-like, and encourage people to read the entire appeal/case for support before deciding to give. This in turn, has the potential to increase donor conversion rate. We chose one email in the campaign to test this concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results in this experiment were flat. We saw .35% decrease in donor conversion rate with a .68% level of confidence. The average gift across both the control and treatment were also negligible (control: $250, treatment: $238) so the humanized treatment did not inspire people to give more either. A concern in removing the buttons from the email was that it would negatively impact click thru rate. However, results indicate that the removal of the buttons has no effect on click thru rate either. Click thru rate stayed the same at .4%.
Where we did see a noticeable difference was the impact on new donors. New donors experienced a 497% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 94%.  More non donors were activated to give through the treatment (no buttons) than the control. While it's not a large number of transactions, the organization should consider using this new layout (no buttons) knowing that it will not impact giving overall but may active more new donors. It's likely that non donors saw the email as less transactional/promotional and decided to read more of the appeal and were therefore more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How a forward style email in a high urgency campaign affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a forward style email  for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
In an effort to continue to humanize communications during a high urgency campaign, we wanted to use a “forward” style email in one of our email appeals. This email would refer to a previous email and make it seem as if the email was forwarded to the recipient. The control for this experiment had the intended, original email (a message from the President). The treatment was also a message from the President but it asked the email recipient to refer to the previous email sent.
In both of these emails previous donors were suppressed so as not to accidentally include those who may have given in the previous email. The email tested was an evening email on the last day of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 3.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6.69%. This means that this experiment had almost no effect on donor conversion rate. The control seemingly has a higher average gift that is skewed by a $3k gift. There is also no noticeable change in click thru rate. Our hypothesis is that because this was tested in an email that's in the "high urgency" phase of the campaign, motivation to give before the deadline is already high. It's recommended that this is retested at a different phase in a campaign to more clearly discern motivation.

experiments.title: How adding an additional and different CTA in the PS of an email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional call to action in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During calendar year end RTB wanted to be sure to let their constituents know about additional giving options for their 70+ aged donors. There was concern that too many CTA’s would interfere with donor conversion rate so we tested the P.S. message across two different emails in order to get more data around this decision. Our hypothesis was that an additional, different CTA would negatively impact donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not see a valid result from this experiment, we did see a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 70%. It's recommended that before rolling this out to additional emails that this is tested across several more emails to get a large enough sample size as the data seems to indicate a decrease in donor conversion rate when adding the different CTA in the P.S. of the email.

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer to a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a thermometer to a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
During Giving Tuesday, RTB wanted to include a progress bar on the on-site sticky bar. We wanted to test adding this thermometer in order to determine if it motivates more people to give through the sticky bar. The results of this experiment also determined if the organization would invest time and labor to manually update a thermometer in the sticky bar during the rest of the calendar year end experiment. If we saw significant gains by adding the thermometer, we would invest the time in manually updating the thermometer.
This sticky bar was live across the RTB site. Traffic was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: We initially believed that the thermometer would provide a visual representation of the organization's goal and would motivate more people to give. Results indicate that the thermometer did not have a significant impact. The experiment resulted in a 42% decrease in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 73%. There weren't many transactions through this experiment because it only ran for a couple dates for Giving Tuesday. However, we determined that because we saw a decrease (though invalid), we would not move forward in adding this feature in the rest of the campaign. It's probable that the goal seemed too large to the site visitor and ultimately made the goal feel unattainable by seeing such a large amount in the thermometer.

experiments.title: How adding a first name personalization on an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a first name personalization for users who requested an offer will achieve an increased donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 10/13/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a free offer for their Genesis One eBook. After requesting the offer on the landing page, the user would then be taken to an instant donation page that reassures them their offer is on the way and then makes a donation ask. We hypothesized that personalizing this headline with the user’s first name would increase the donor conversion rate. For two reasons: first, using a first name establishes a more personal connection between the organization and the donor; second, it communicates more gratitude. Saying “Thank you,” and ‘Thank you, [First Name]” read very differently.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for nearly 90 days, but with only 31 conversions total the results could not reach validity. As a result, despite a slight decrease in donor conversion rate of -9.4%, the low confidence of 21.8% requires more testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
-9.4% decrease in conversion rate across all traffic.55-64 visitors had a 4.3% increase in donations with an 85% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using a homepage takeover vs a pop up impacted giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a home page takeover during a high urgency campaigh for website visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2022 - 07/04/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign (Fiscal Year End) and wanted to drive more donations during the last week of the campaign. In previous campaigns RTB has done a homepage takeover or a pop up at the end of the campaign to direct website traffic to the donation page. In order to discern which method works best for their audience we decided to run a test where half of their site visitors would get a homepage takeover and the other half would get a pop up. Our initial hypothesis is that more web visitors would convert to donors on a homepage takeover because it takes up more real estate and in turn draws more attention to the campaign. A pop up can be easily dismissed on a webpage.
experiments.keyLearnings: When looking at all traffic, this experiment resulted in a 21% lift in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 40%.  When we observe only new visitors we see a higher level of confidence: 82% lift with a level of confidence of 77%. While this did not validate, it is probable that new visitors to the site may have been confused by seeing an entirely new landing page (the Fiscal Year End donation page) and therefore responded more to the pop up. However, since this experiment only ran for the last week of the campaign, we did not see sufficient enough traffic to validate the results. It is recommended that this is retested over a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a ebook offer to the homepage affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a free ebook offer for site visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/20/2022
Reasons to Believe has had a generic newsletter sign up at the bottom of their homepage. In a previous experiment, we added more clarity in the language to increase sign up to their newsletter. When that did not result in a lift in email acquisition, we decided to try a more radical approach. In order to inspire more email subscriptions, we replaced the newsletter subscription with an ebook offer. Wanting to reduce friction, we embedded the actual sign up form in this content block so that the site visitor did not have to visit another page to get this offer. Once people filled out the form, they were directed to an instant donation page.
Traffic to this site was primarily organic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for nearly a month, we saw no real impact in email conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 1% decrease in email conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 6%. This means that the user was not motivated to give more based off of an offer. Given that the form as at the very bottom of the homepage could indicate that only those who are more invested in their site (enough to scroll all the way down) were already looking to take a next step--whether that was signing up for their newsletter or getting an ebook. We saw no difference in new visitors vs returning visitors. Nor did we see a difference in donation activity.
It's suggested that we optimize the newsletter sign up form by focusing on a different aspect of the value proposition, like exclusivity. In addition to that, we can test the offer once more but making it a slide out pop up so that the user does not have to scroll all the way to the bottom of the page to take advantage of this offer.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array from a donation page impacts revenue during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the gift array from the campaign donation page for page visitors  will achieve increased conversions and higher revenue per visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2021 - 07/09/2021
RTB’s donation pages always have a gift array to offer a preselected giving amount for the site visitor. For RTB’s 2021 FYE campaign, we wanted to test removing the gift array on the campaign donation page to see how this would impact giving (donations and revenue). The idea behind it is that visitors would give more without being guided by a gift array and having clear direction to choose their own giving amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While revenue only decreased 6.7% with a level of confidence of 55%, donor conversion rate had a directional decrease of 11.9% (LoC 88%). Though the data is not statistically valid, the results seem to indicate the the gift array is helpful in providing people a guide on how much they should give. If they aren't given pre-selected amounts, they are less likely to give on the donation page. It is recommended that further testing is done on the gift array. Changing the amounts, the ascending/descending order and the placement in relation to the one-time/monthly option are all potential next steps for testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 101.6% increase in revenue with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 139.4% increase in revenue with an 89% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 131.7% increase in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the gift array  for donation page visitors will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2021 - 07/29/2021
RTB had previously tested removing the gift array from a donation page during a high urgency campaign but the results were inconclusive. In order to try and garner valid results, they retested this concept again at another point of their high urgency campaign, on a different donation page. The hypothesis is that removing the gift array from the donation page may increase revenue and/or donor conversion rate as prospective donors feel like they have more autonomy in the giving process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the gift array resulted in fewer conversions and a decrease in revenue. The experiment results indicate that donation page visitors give more and give greater when they have a gift array available for guidance. A gift array takes some of the decision out of play for potential donors; instead of a donor having to think of a giving amount on their own, they instead have options presented to them, easing their decision-making.
Furthermore, removing the gift array resulted in lower revenue. This shows that a donor's "best gift" is often lower than what it would be if

experiments.title: How adding a thermometer and value proposition language impacts donor conversion in a higher urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity through value proposition language and motivation through a giving thermometer for potential monthly donors will achieve increased monthly donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2021 - 10/01/2021
RTB was running their monthly partner campaign in which their goal was to grow their number of monthly donors. We ran a test on RTB’s monthly partner donation page where two variables were tested.
We added value proposition language to each monthly giving button/option. The thought was that we could increase donor conversion rate by providing clarity on what their gift is going towards.
We also added a thermometer that indicated new monthly donors compared to RTB’s monthly partner campaign goal. We thought that adding clarity through value prop to what different levels of monthly giving can provide in addition to a visible goal that visitors can contribute to would lead to more monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in fewer conversions than the control; however, the difference was marginal.  This experiment resulted in a 68% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 70%. Therefore the results of this experiment did not validate. The run time of the experiment was only a week and could have validated given more time. However, there were two vastly different concepts/variables tested in this experiment. It is recommended that this is retested by isolating a single variable. From this experiment, we don't know if the fewer conversions were a result of the thermometer, added value prop, or both.

experiments.title: Does adding a security box around payment fields on a donation page affect revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a security box around the payment information section of the donation form for donation page visitors will achieve greater revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2021 - 10/30/2021
On RTB’s main donation page, we thought that adding a secure symbol and a security box around the payment information part of the donation page would encourage page visitors to give and to give more. While transactions to this page are already secure, this visual representation can encourage more donations as prospective donors feel like their payment information is safe with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a lengthy period of time, the results still did not validate. We saw a 14% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 65%.
The security box around the payment information portion of the donation form resulted in lower revenue/visitor and lower average gift. The appeal of a "secure" checkout did not encourage donors to give more.
One can conclude that RTB donors already trust the organization and adding a secure box did not persuasde or dissuade them enough to give.
An additional test idea would be to add a security box around the entire donation form. Perhaps if donation page visitors saw the secure symbol earlier in the process they would be encouraged to give and to give more.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to an email subscription form impacts email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to an email subscription form for website visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2022 - 04/17/2022
Reasons to Believe has a form on their homepage to capture emails for their newsletter subscription. In order to increase name acquisition we decided to add clarity to the language on the form. The original form informed people to “sign up for our newsletter” without detailing what value the recipient was getting. We adjusted the language so there was more clarity on what they were getting and at what frequency. We also added first name and last name fields because this information was not being captured in their original form.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment did not validate, we were seeing a 14% decrease in name acquisition with a 76% level of confidence. The experiment had already been running for a month without much difference in metrics. The perceived decrease in name acquisition could be due to the fact that we added additional fields to this form. It may be better to split this out into 2 different experiments (one focused on language and the other focused on fields) in order to isolate the variables appropriately. We could also test into different aspects of the value proposition. We tested into clarity but our next step can be to test into exclusivity.

experiments.title: How using an offer that is relevant to the author of a blog affects email acquisition in a dear reader (Pt.3)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an offer in a dear reader that is relevant to the author for readers will achieve a higher email conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2022 - 04/10/2022
Reasons to Believe is a science-faith organization that has a plethora of blog content that receives a lot of traffic. They also have specific scholars/authors who have their own blogs and articles. We wanted to see if using an offer in a dear reader that is specific and relevant to the author will yield a higher email conversion rate. In this particular case we were targeting Ken Samples’ articles. In this experiment we used an evangelism survey as the control and an eBook authored by Ken Samples as the treatment.
All traffic for this experiment is from organic web traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using the eBook that was related to the author produced no difference in email conversion rate or any other metric. This is inconsistent with the previous experiment's (Hugh Ross' blog/dear reader and Fuz Rana's blog/dear reader) findings.
We hypothesize that this difference in results is due to the difference in audience. Hugh and Fuz's blogs are much more scientific so their readers are more likely to gravitate towards an eBook with scientific content than an evangelism survey. Whereas Ken's blogs are theology/philosophy based so it is likely that this audience will respond to either an evangelism survey or an eBook from Ken.
We are following up with an experiment that uses the ebook dear reader and uses first person language so it sounds like it's from the author himself.

experiments.title: How reducing gift array amounts affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array amounts for main donate page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2021 - 01/27/2022
RTB’s main donate page begins its gift array at $500 which is significantly higher than other organization’s gift arrays. We hypothesize that by reducing the gift array amounts (starting the array at $250 instead), we’ll see a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though this experiment ran for a significant amount of time in order to get a larger sample size, this experiment did not validate. We did not see a directional lift in either donor conversion rate or revenue.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 19.4% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 28.2% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 63.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a blog description above an article can lead to an increase in clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the article descriptions above the article image  for the highlight section in the newsletter will achieve an increase in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2021
At the bottom of RTB’s weekly newsletter email, they would have a “highlights” section where they would link to other articles/blogs. Below the articles, they would have a paragraph that would go into detail about what each blog/article was. The hypothesis was if we move the detailed description of what each article was directly above the article it would then lead to more clicks.
This was set up as a 50/50 split and was sent to their entire newsletter subscription file.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall this experiment was a wash. We saw a slight increase in clicks but this experiment was nowhere close to validating. While this experiment didn't validate we did see that adding the description above the articles didn't lead to a decrease in clicks so we are looking to test this again.
